title: EXISTS Operator
content:
### EXISTS Operator

EXISTS checks whether a subquery returns any rows. It returns TRUE if the subquery has at least one row, FALSE otherwise.

---

### EXISTS Syntax

```sql
SELECT columns
FROM table
WHERE EXISTS (subquery);
```

### Basic Example

```sql
-- Find customers who have placed at least one order
SELECT c.name, c.email
FROM customers c
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM orders o
    WHERE o.customer_id = c.id
);
```

### NOT EXISTS

```sql
-- Find customers who have NOT placed any orders
SELECT c.name, c.email
FROM customers c
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM orders o
    WHERE o.customer_id = c.id
);
```

### EXISTS vs IN

| Aspect | EXISTS | IN |
|--------|--------|----|
| Performance | Better for large subquery results | Better for small, static lists |
| NULL handling | Not affected by NULLs | NOT IN fails if list contains NULL |
| Subquery dependency | Often correlated | Usually non-correlated |
| Return value | Boolean (TRUE/FALSE) | Value comparison |

### SELECT 1 Convention

By convention, SELECT 1 is used inside EXISTS subqueries because the actual columns don't matter:

```sql
-- These are equivalent:
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders WHERE customer_id = c.id)
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = c.id)
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 'anything' FROM orders WHERE customer_id = c.id)
```

> **Key Point:** EXISTS is often faster than IN for large subquery results because it stops scanning as soon as it finds a match. It also handles NULL values correctly.
codeExample:
SELECT p.product_name
FROM products p
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM order_items oi
    WHERE oi.product_id = p.id
      AND oi.quantity > 10
);

quiz.question: What does EXISTS return?
options: TRUE if subquery has any rows, FALSE otherwise

quiz.question: Why is SELECT 1 commonly used inside EXISTS?
options: The actual columns do not matter for EXISTS
It is required by SQL standard
